speaker: interviewer
text: Sabina Callwood. Interviewer, Sontha Strinko. City, Miami. Country, USA. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take one. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: On behalf of the survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation, I'd like to introduce Ms. Sabina Callwood. Today is March 3, 1995. And my name is Sontha Strinko. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It will go away once the sun--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, roughly.

speaker: interviewer
text: --yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you please state your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sabina Callwood, and my maiden name is Wagschal, W-A-G-S-C-H-A-L. And my married name, Callwood, is spelled C-A-L-L-W-O-O-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Antwerp, in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how old are you today, mam?

speaker: interviewee
text: May-- May 8, I will be exactly 59 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'd like to start out with the prewar experience. What type of kid were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was always a responsible child. From very early onset, I realized that a caretaker program was set up for my persona because of the dependency that had been laid out in front of me by my parents. I undertook that without questions. And I always would watch my two brothers and make sure that my mother was happy, my father was happy-- kind of took elements of-- of strains away from people whenever I could, from early on. So I was already an old child, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the oldest child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was definitely the oldest by living, whereas my mother had one previous child who died at six months. And his name was Israel Wagschal. But I'm the oldest of the-- of the remaining five-- meaning, of the remaining four-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brothers like and your relationship with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my next- of-- the next sibling is my brother, Samuel Aron Wagschal. He was a darling boy-- wonderful to be with. And the next one was Jack Wagschal-- no middle name. And he was absolutely adorable-- sweet as honey. And both of my brothers, I adore them today. They are alive. They're married. They have children. They have weddings. And they are gorgeous people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much older were you from your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: One year of Samuel and three years-- almost three years of my brother Jack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you in this caretaker position in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think sometimes we are destined to receive a certain role in life that we have no control over. It-- it is something that I did not ask for, but it just happened by accident. And I've never questioned it. I've accepted it. And I carried it until this time, at this present. I'm very much concerned continuously with the welfare of children, specifically because children is the future. They are the hope and the inspiration of continuation of life. And children need to be loved, disciplined, and respected. I don't see it too much today, unfortunately. Children have gone into-- for that matter, the way I see right now, what is happening to children, is that children almost have to go into hiding places. Where I used to hide to survive from a Holocaust-- I see today, in our cities of the United States, that children almost have to go in hiding, so they should not get molested or killed. This is a very, very sad day to discover that something like that goes on in the finest country in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother and your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father were second cousins. My father was born and raised in Krosno, Poland. And my mother was raised and born in Bremen, Germany. But their heritage, on my mother's side, was first born and raised in Poland. They migrated to Germany, and that's where my mother was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am also proud to say that I am-- how we should we say that? My forefather was the man who was born in 1707. I discovered that two years ago-- who has a book known as [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was a mystical rabbi in Poland, and he wrote about life and living. It is this book up to today that is reverent in the Hasidic world.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my late grandfather, the only one who was really able to survive the Holocaust, but died an early death in New York City, he came here because people wanted him here-- Yehoshua Wagschal-- Rabbi Yehoshua Wagschal. He had the manuscripts of the forefather's life and living, reissued it into a book, and it came out in 1942. And thereafter, several other people also published it. But it's known as [NON-ENGLISH]. And I'm very proud to carry that heritage around me now. His daughter married into the Wagschal family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was on your father's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my father's side. But whatever is on my father's side is also on my mother side, since their-- since their mothers were first cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Salomon Wagschal, [NON-ENGLISH] in Jewish, was an extraordinary human being who had a will for life, almost willfully so. And he was a very talented diamond cutter. But prior to that, he used to be a farrier, but he liked diamond cutting better. And he was a joker. He loved to joke. He sang a good [NON-ENGLISH] on Shabbos night dinner. And he was all around a very interesting human being who had a great passion for philosophy also. I learned a lot from him when it came to understanding the little intricacies of life and living. And my mother-- my mother was a darling person. Her name is Yetta Wagschal, and her maiden name was Yetta [NON-ENGLISH]. And she was-- even my husband Tony, when he met her, he says, you know, Sabina, it's not often I meet a lady who still blushes. And my mother always used to blush. She was a dainty-- almost a-- a German-Japanese-style female, if I can say so. And she loved dancing, music, poetry-- had a knack for just enjoying the very artistic part of life. They were interesting. And life was not easy. There was turbulence in the family. And I tried to overcome that before the war and after the war. But I think that each negativity that comes along the way, we should have the courage and the understanding to turn it into a positive. Because we who continue must always continue for the better, and not for the worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the negativities that went on in your home before the war and how old you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as you know, in most domestic worlds, there are peaceful worlds and not such peaceful worlds. And unfortunately, in my family life, there was a lot of breakdowns of communications. And when you have breakdowns of communication, not understanding one with the other, there seems to be always an issue of heated elements-- which, as a child, leaves a certain-- you-- you become-- I-- I did-- I-- it did not affect me to become negative. Contraire, it allowed me to go deeper within my own thoughts to analyze why two people cannot live in peace together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as you know, I am now married-- in May, 34 years. And I will say, rating from a 1 to a 10, we have achieved a 9, which is pretty high today, isn't it, Sontha? I learned to undo-- to do better-- from observing and experiencing firsthand. What did you observe and experience-- at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I would want to really go into details, because both my parents are not here. They're not alive. And may they rest in peace. I love them, and I always will love them. But I think that sometimes things happen in human being's life that they don't ask for. It just happen because two elements did not really fit into the groove. And you know what goes on in-- nowadays in family life. And I feel bad for every woman, for every child, for every man that goes through the stresses of family life. It needn't be. Because we can absolutely bring ourselves into communication to make it better. But I would like to share with you, Sontha, an experience during the course of the prewar. When the war had begun, we were still living at a place known as [NON-ENGLISH], Antwerpen. We lived on the third floor-- no elevator. But the third floor was almost like six floors here. We had two rooms and a toilet on the outside, in the hallway. And one night, we heard very loud banging at the front door downstairs. And we all were like, trembling, you know, because we knew. We had heard the sirens, and so forth. And the Gestapo came upstairs, forcefully knocked on the door. And they told my father to get ready in the middle of the night. Unless you go through this, to see your father being taken away for no reason at all, but just to be taken away-- my mother became hysterical. She was a nervous wreck. She developed Crohn disease, bleeding ulcers, and she could not concentrate and help no more her three children because of her illnesses. She had six major operations during her lifetime. We were then brought-- because my mother was taken to a hospital-- and we, the three children, were then taken away from that domicile.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was six to six and 1/2, if I-- I think I was closer to six than six and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year that was about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in '36, so we're talking anywhere-- the latter part of '42. And I will say this-- that when you hear the constant sirens and the bombs, you who are standing there in your domicile, you have no-- you-- you don't know what to do. You're totally, completely powerless. It is this powerlessness that caused us all to have so many diseases because of the fear. You'd be amazed that fright can kill. But we couldn't allow ourselves to get killed. We had to survive. But we would get all kinds of problems when we are-- were having these fears. When we were separated from my mother, we never saw her again until 1945. So my years as a child were-- before the war-- were extremely traumatic, unhappy, and in a sense, hopeful for a better life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your town like before the war? Did you have much activities with other people, like, other friends, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never-- I never had friends. I was not allowed to bring, even after the war, a girlfriend to the house. No, we were pretty much isolated from outsiders as children. And the activities was always centered around my parents. Life was around the family and just being there. There was nothing there. I'll tell you one thing. We never had a toy. We never had a single toy. So we didn't have anything to play with.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember that we would go to that toilet seat-- I don't know why we did this at the time-- and just play with the toilet seat, which was wood. And it had kind of splinters. And we'd always splinter our fingers. And to-- even till today, I don't understand why we played with a toilet seat-- that this was the greatest enjoyment for us is to play with a toilet seat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when my mother would go shopping, she would place my brothers-- one alongside of my little person-- on a large trunk that was in the hallway, made of wicker. And I would be a babysitter. My brother would be one-years-old, and my other one would be two and 1/2, and I will be going on four.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were there, and I-- that's why-- what-- what I'm set from the beginning. That's why I never questioned. I understood already from early on that I bestowed to be a person to take care. So when my mother went, she had no fear. Because she knew she could depend that I was watching those two little boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I adore them. They're just wonderful. So there were good times and bad times. I have many memories, which I don't want to-- really, they're rather personal that happened there. But for every negativity, I have learned a positive. I refuse to go with the negative.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any religious activities observed in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father was, at that time, Orthodox Jew. And we were kosher all the way. But in the latter years, my father became extremely, extremely Hasidic-- religious. And my sister, at present, 1995, is a Hasidic woman. Yes. She's very, very religious and is very observant in every aspect of Jewish life. And her name is Sara-Golde Wagschal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you told as a child about Hitler and about Nazis? And did you understand what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I truly did not-- not then. This only came to me when I was sent to that home where we spent the time with Madam Rothschild. If you want-- do you want me to go into that segment with Madam Rothschild?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell us how you got-- why you were separated from your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my father was taken away, and-- by the Gestapo. And by the way, he was placed in a French labor camp in France. And I have some stories to tell about that one. And my mother, being very, very, very sick, had to be taken to a hospital. And she continued to be in the hospital. So we had underground people, Jewish underground people, as well as non-Jewish Belgium women, who took every child that was unwillingly temporarily orphaned, to group them into certain houses for safety. And this is how my two brothers and myself came into the house of Madam Rosie Rothschild. This lady is also one of the reasons, besides my parents, why I am presenting this story. She saved my life. And it is in this house that I learned, finally, what the war was all about. Madam Rosie Rothschild was the directress of a home of children who were being hidden from the Gestapo. The Germans knew of her, but for some reason, they still let her be. It is not-- it wasn't always that way. But in that moment, she was still able to save children. And we were 144 strong-- if I'm not mistaken-- children that were being placed. And the day when my brother Sam, and Jack, and myself went to her, we three-- and a little boy, and another little girl by the name of Anneke-- we were standing there, watching 60 children being taken away by the Gestapo. So we stood there, and it was empty suddenly-- all the children gone. And then came a whole new group of children. It is in that house that I learned the true fear of life as a hidden child in Belgium. And there are certain stories I like to tell about this particular residence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this residence?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [NON-ENGLISH], Antwerpen. It was a large, official building right on the street-- main entrance and a side entrance. The side entrance led to a school known as [NON-ENGLISH] School, a Jewish school. The school itself and the building had a common backyard. It also had, on this side, a library of books by the hundreds-- all Hebrew Jewish books. And one day, they came to ransack the whole school. And they took the hundreds of books, threw them out of the window in that yard-- while we stood there and watched-- and lit a bonfire on all these books. During that same day, as I'm watching, I'm holding this little boy, the one that previously stood besides me when the other children were taken away. And one person threw a stone at us. Now please understand that in Belgium, there were two types of people. There were the people who sacrificed and helped many Jews-- the non-Jews I'm speaking of now. And there was a group of Belgium, non-Jews, who for every Jew they delivered to the Nazis, they would get paid. What happened is they were known, believe it or not, as the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the blacks. And it is those pro-Nazi helpers that did a lot of damages locally to us little children. We were mocked. We were-- we were beaten. We were hit, et cetera, et cetera. But we survived each and every time.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is in that place that I started to dislike a vegetable known as turnips. Because that was, in that particular place, one of the main foods that was given to us. Have you ever taken a turnip, boil it, no salt, no nothing, and eat it like this? Yuck. So until today, I never eat turnips, and I'm a vegetarian. But thank God he created so many beautiful fruits and vegetables. So there are choices galore. What was your daily routine like as a child in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The daily routine-- you got up, and you prayed that you could make the night-- so you can survive. And at night, when the sirens happened and the bombs were falling-- one night, I unfortunately I had dysentery. And the whole night they were working on my little body to make sure that I survive. And I survived. It was very, very frightening experience. Because when your body is dehydrating like this, and in an atmosphere where you didn't have the tools to work with, somehow they found a way to keep me alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Madam Rothschild and several other people there who-- because you have to keep washing, and cleaning, and et cetera. I also want to indicate that my brother, Sam-- I call him Sammy-- he was taken away-- both my brothers-- after three months staying with Madam Rothschild-- were separated from me and then from each other. Sammy was taken to a place known as Wezembeek. This was a very large place-- one building-- of which there were a little over 100 children. They used to go to school there. The Germans came and forcibly closed the school and told them that they must go to the Catholic school. So they had to wear the khaki-- how do you say that-- cape-- with the yellow star, which I did likewise. And I wore also klompen-- the wooden shoes. And they had to march 30 minutes each day to that school to be able to have some kind of education. But then something happened. They had to close the whole home, because the Gestapo was ready to take the children away. You see, the underground was always working to make sure that all of us knew beforehand if we could stay in a place or not stay in a place. So when-- when that happened, they closed the home, and non-Jewish Belgium women started to help in the Jewish underground to organize small groups of children, to place them in different homes, temporarily, to hide them. My brother Sammy and two other boys were sent to a Catholic priest home. There they lived with this priest for over six months. After that, they came back to Wezembeek. And this is where Sammy stayed till the end of the war. And I want to indicate. He told me that when he was in Wezembeek, one of the things that he remembers so well are two things. One, in the courtyard, they used to strategically place mattresses-- not one after the other, but in different areas-- so that if anything should come from the sky, the children could run under the mattresses. The other thing he remembered was-- and he said that devastated him-- when a German pointed a rifle when he was standing at the window, right to the window where he was standing. That devastated him terribly. Whereas my brother, Jacky, he was sent-- and I believe to Linkebeek. But in his case, he never stayed in one place like Sammy. Sammy was in two places-- the Catholic priest for six months, a little over that, and all the time in Wezembeek. But Jacky told me that he was every few weeks in another place, and many times had to spend his life in the forest-- in the bushes-- and just pick something from the earth to survive. So his life was extremely traumatic for a three, four-year-old to-- running. We who were six and seven, we were considered the oldest ones. And we had to take care of the three and four-year-old ones. So we were already so old. We did not know our childhood. We never had one. And so he somehow survived this constant shuffling from one place to another. It was almost every few weeks he was moved around. But he made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you were separated from your brothers at first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I-- I was so unhappy, because remember, mother is gone, father is gone. And now my two brothers and myself, we are there three months. And now we are separated after three months. I suddenly was alone. But Madam Rothschild was such a sweet, and loving, and beautiful human being. I really grew to love her as my own. And I honor her. Each--
